title: Lipo-glycopeptide cleavable derivatives and uses thereof
abstract: The present invention provides certain lipo-glycopeptide cleavable derivatives and methods for using the same for the treatment of bacterial infections, for example, pulmonary bacterial infections. The LGPC derivatives include a cleavable moiety that in certain embodiments, is designed to allow for cellular uptake and/or a more rapid clearance of the glycopeptide metabolite (i.e., the cleaved glycopeptide) from the site of administration (e.g., the lung) as compared to the uncleaved LGPC.

A method of treating a bacterial infection in a patient in need of treatment, comprising administering to the patient a composition comprising an effective amount of a lipo- glycopeptide cleavable (LGPC) derivative, or a pharmaceutically acceptable salt thereof, wherein the LGPC is a glycopeptide comprising a primary amino conjugated lipophilic moiety, and wherein the lipophilic moiety is conjugated to the primary amino group via a functional group that can undergo enzymatic hydrolysis. 71. The method of claim 70, wherein the functional group is conjugated to the primary amino group of the glycopeptide via a straight chain or branched chain alkyl group. 72. The method of claim 70 or 71, wherein the LGPC derivative has a faster rate of clearance at the site of administration compared to a glycopeptide having the same glycopeptide core structure as the LGPC, and a non-cleavable lipophilic moiety conjugated to the primary amino group. 73. The method of any one of claims 70-72, wherein the LGPC derivative has a shorter half-life (T1/2) at the site of administration compared to a glycopeptide having the same glycopeptide core structure as the LGPC, and a non-cleavable lipophilic moiety conjugated to the primary amino group. 74. A method of treating a bacterial infection in a patient in need of treatment, comprising administering to the patient a composition comprising an effective amount of compound of any one of claims 1-69, or a pharmaceutically acceptable salt thereof. 75. The method of any one of claims 70-74, wherein the composition is in the form of nanoparticles. 76. The method of claim 75, wherein the nanoparticles comprise a biodegradable polymer. 77. The method of claim 76, wherein the biodegradable polymer is poly(D,L-lactide), poly(lactic acid) (PLA), poly(D,L-glycolide) (PLG), poly(lactide-co-glycolide) (PLGA), poly- (cyanoacrylate) (PCA), or a combination thereof. 78. The method of claim 76, wherein the biodegradable polymer is poly(lactic-co-glycolitic acid) (PLGA). 79. The method of any one of claims 75-78, wherein the mean diameter of the nanoparticles is from about 50 nm to about 900 nm. 80. The method of claim 79, wherein the mean diameter of the nanoparticles is from about 100 nm to about 500 nm. 81. The method of any one of claims 70-80, wherein the composition comprises a phospholipid and a polyvalent cation. 82. The method of claim 81, wherein the phospholipid is a saturated phosphatidylcholine. 83. The method of claim 81 or 82, wherein the polyvalent cation is in the form of a hygroscopic salt. 84. The method of any one of claims 70-83, wherein the bacterial infection is a planktonic bacterial infection, a bacterial biofilm infection, or a combination thereof. 85. The method of any one of claims 70-84, wherein the bacterial infection is a bacterial biofilm infection. 86. The method of any one of claims 70-85, wherein the administering is via the intravenous route. 87. The method of any one of claims 70-85, wherein the bacterial infection is a bacterial pulmonary infection. 88.
